---
title: "Salary & Compensation trends"
format: html
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Research Questions
This section focuses on the following questions:

1. How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers?
2. What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
3. Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
4. How did salary levels change over time in 2024?
5. What industries saw the biggest wage growth in 2024?
6. Which job characteristics are most strongly associated with salary?
7. Can machine learning predict whether a job is high-paying?

## Load Data

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import plotly.express as px
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report

df = pd.read_csv("data/processed/lightcast_cleaned.csv")
df.head()

df["POSTED"] = pd.to_datetime(df["POSTED"], errors="coerce")
df["post_month"] = df["POSTED"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str)
df["post_year"] = df["POSTED"].dt.year
```

## 1. How do salaries differ across AI vs. non-AI careers
```{python}
ai_keywords = [
    "ai", "artificial intelligence", "machine learning", "ml",
    "deep learning", "nlp", "computer vision", "generative ai",
    "data scientist", "machine learning engineer", "ai engineer"
]

df["TITLE_CLEAN"] = df["TITLE_CLEAN"].astype(str)

df["ai_flag"] = df["TITLE_CLEAN"].str.lower().apply(
    lambda x: "AI-related" if any(k in x for k in ai_keywords) else "Non-AI"
)

salary_ai = (
    df.groupby("ai_flag")["salary_annual"]
      .mean()
      .reset_index()
      .sort_values("salary_annual", ascending=False)
)

fig = px.bar(
    salary_ai,
    x="ai_flag",
    y="salary_annual",
    title="Average Annual Salary: AI-related vs. Non-AI Careers",
    labels={"ai_flag": "Career Type", "salary_annual": "Average Annual Salary"}
)
fig.show()
```
The bar chart shows that AI-related careers have a slightly higher average annual salary than non-AI careers. 
In this sample, the average salary for AI-related roles is around 120k, while non-AI roles are slightly lower at about 116k. 
This suggests that jobs involving AI and machine learning skills tend to be compensated at a somewhat higher level.

However, the salary gap is not very large, which may indicate that many non-AI positions in the dataset are also high-paying technical or business roles. 
Overall, the figure supports the idea that AI-related jobs offer a salary premium, but the difference appears to be moderate rather than dramatic.


## 2. What regions offer the highest-paying jobs in AI-related and traditional careers?
```{python}
region_salary = (
    df.groupby(["STATE_NAME", "ai_flag"])["salary_annual"]
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

top_regions = (
    region_salary.groupby("STATE_NAME")["salary_annual"]
    .mean()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(10)
    .index
)

region_salary_top = region_salary[region_salary["STATE_NAME"].isin(top_regions)]

fig = px.bar(
    region_salary_top.sort_values("salary_annual", ascending=True),
    x="salary_annual",
    y="STATE_NAME",
    color="ai_flag",
    barmode="group",
    orientation="h",
    title="Top Regions by Average Salary: AI-related vs. Non-AI Careers",
    labels={
        "STATE_NAME": "Region",
        "salary_annual": "Average Annual Salary",
        "ai_flag": "Career Type"
    }
)

fig.show()
```

The chart shows that salary levels vary noticeably across regions, confirming that geography is an important factor in compensation. Among the regions shown, Montana has the highest average salary for AI-related jobs, making it the strongest-paying region for AI-related careers in this comparison. In general, AI-related jobs tend to pay more than non-AI jobs across most of the top-paying regions, suggesting that AI and technical skills receive an additional salary premium in many local labor markets.

At the same time, the size of the salary gap differs across regions, which indicates that the value of AI-related skills is not uniform across locations. Overall, the figure suggests that both region and career type jointly influence salary outcomes.

## 3. Are remote jobs better paying than in-office roles?
```{python}
remote_salary = (
    df[df["REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"].notna()]
    .groupby("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME")["salary_annual"]
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values("salary_annual", ascending=False)
)

fig = px.bar(
    remote_salary,
    x="REMOTE_TYPE_NAME",
    y="salary_annual",
    title="Average Annual Salary by Work Arrangement",
    labels={
        "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME": "Work Arrangement",
        "salary_annual": "Average Annual Salary"
    }
)

fig.show()
```
The chart shows that fully remote jobs have the highest average annual salary, followed by hybrid remote roles, while not remote jobs have the lowest average salary among the three categories. This suggests that remote work is associated with higher-paying positions in this dataset.

However, this result should be interpreted carefully. The higher average salary for remote jobs may reflect the fact that many remote positions are concentrated in high-paying technical or analytical occupations, rather than remote work itself directly causing higher pay. Overall, the figure indicates a positive association between remote work arrangements and salary levels, but other factors such as industry, role type, and experience may also be important.



```{python}
df["POSTED"] = pd.to_datetime(df["POSTED"], errors="coerce")
df["post_month"] = df["POSTED"].dt.to_period("M").astype(str)
df["post_year"] = df["POSTED"].dt.year

salary_time = (
    df[df["post_year"] == 2024]
    .groupby(["post_month", "ai_flag"])["salary_annual"]
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)

fig = px.line(
    salary_time,
    x="post_month",
    y="salary_annual",
    color="ai_flag",
    markers=True,
    title="Monthly Average Salary in 2024: AI-related vs. Non-AI Careers",
    labels={
        "post_month": "Month",
        "salary_annual": "Average Annual Salary",
        "ai_flag": "Career Type"
    }
)

fig.show()
```
The line chart shows that AI-related jobs generally maintained higher average salaries than non-AI jobs during most months in 2024. While both groups experienced a decline around July 2024, salaries rose again afterward, especially for AI-related roles. From July to September, the salary gap widened noticeably, with AI-related careers showing stronger upward growth than non-AI careers.

This pattern suggests that the salary premium for AI-related jobs was not only persistent over time but also became more pronounced toward the later months in the dataset. Overall, the time analysis indicates that AI-related roles experienced greater salary growth and stronger momentum than non-AI roles in 2024.



```{python}

```


```{python}

```
